original_abstract:
In *Rethinking Global Health*, Dr. Rochelle Burgess offers a critical examination of the hidden mechanics of power and their tangible consequences within the landscape of international health. Moving beyond mere theoretical critique, the book addresses two fundamental questions: who is global health actually designed for, and what structural barriers prevent us from creating healthcare responses that truly resonate with people’s lived realities?

Drawing on a decade of experience as a scholar-activist and community health psychologist, Burgess weaves together rigorous power analyses with personal reflections and interviews. The text traverses diverse terrains—from the complexities of global mental health to the frontlines of the West African Ebola crisis and the cholera epidemic in Haiti—to demonstrate how the pursuit of "global solutions" frequently bypasses the very communities they intend to serve.

A cornerstone of this work is the introduction of new analytical tools, notably an adaptation of Patricia Hill Collins’ ‘matrix of domination’ tailored for global health practice. Through this lens, Burgess proposes a shift toward "Transformative Global Health." This framework demands that researchers and practitioners adopt a new set of orienting principles: relinquishing traditional top-down authority and placing community interests, local expertise, and grassroots voices at the absolute center of health planning.

*Rethinking Global Health* is essential reading for academics and students in public health, international relations, and sociology. It also serves as a provocative call to action for activists, practitioners, and anyone committed to dismantling the systemic inequities that persist in the global pursuit of health justice.
humanized_abstract:
In Rethinking Global Health, Rochelle Burgess—a Global Health physician with more than twenty years of international experience—delves beneath the surface to reveal how the distribution of power influences not only its manifestation but also its material effects in the global health arena. Through meticulous ethnographic research into activities in Kenya, India, China, Cameroon, Peru and the U.S., the book answers two of the most important questions that exist in global health today: who does the system of global health exist to serve, and what structural forces make programs feel irrelevant or irrelevant in some degree to the people whose lives they are meant to change.

Drawing on a decade of work as a scholar-activist and community health psychologist, Burgess applies power analyses to her own research and activism, and brings in the perspectives of others through quotes from her interviews. Along the way she moves through a range of landscapes, from critiques of global mental health to the battle to contain the Ebola outbreak in West Africa, to the devastation caused by cholera in Haiti. All these places share one thing in common: the push for “global solutions” and “interventions” that too often leave the communities they aim to help out of the conversation entirely.

This paper sets out to attempt an ambitious task by presenting a change of analytic tools that perhaps signals an effort to see the world from a very different place and thereby establish the initial conditions required to create "Transformative Global Health". With these new tools at hand – where a Matrix of Domination adapted to Global Health developed by Burgess with particular respect to the work of Collins (2000), functions as an axis around which the discussion and conceptual developments revolve – a series of reflections are prompted by an abrupt demand for change in the world view that underpins research and development efforts. The researcher and the practicing international health community is challenged with abandoning what amounts to an ‘almost omniscient view’ of reality: the struggle to achieve ‘better health’ can only be considered sustainable where the changing terms of this bargaining process – and the relevant conditions – see the needs of people and the perspectives and competence of those nearest to where health work will be undertaken, given prevalence and immediacy; situated centre stage in what becomes a radical re-configuration of who has influence, power or command, in international health activities that recognize and give weight to critical awareness, diversity, reciprocity as matters for consideration as one embarks upon interventions to improve health status, inequality and participation, globally and within different societies, where all actions presume an equitable relationship between ‘who receives’ and ‘who brings’, assistance and resources in health for those who need them, regardless of social status.

In an increasingly interconnected world, Rethinking Global Health is an important collection of scholarship that is relevant to students, scholars, and practitioners of global health, international relations, and sociology, as well as to health activists and advocates seeking to address pressing health disparities and create a more equitable world.
